hero.description: An immersive journey through 45 years of life behind the Iron Curtain.

timeline.title: 45 Years Behind the
timeline.description: From the Soviet-backed coup in 1944 to the fall of the regime in 1989, Bulgaria remained one of the most loyal allies of the USSR.
timeline.quote: Those who do not learn History are doomed to repeat it
timeline.disclaimer: This website has been designated and registered online for cultural and historical purposes. It is in no way the tool of any political ideology.

coup1944.title: The Coup of September 9
coup1944.description: Communist-led partisans, armed by the British SOE and supported by the USSR, overthrow the royalist government allied with Nazi Germany. The Red Army had entered Bulgarian territory three days earlier.

peoplesCourt.title: People's Court Trials
peoplesCourt.description: Over 2,700 'war criminals' sentenced to death including regents, ministers, and MPs.

referendum.title: Republic Referendum
referendum.description: Bulgaria declared a people's republic following the referendum.

dimitrovConstitution.title: Dimitrov Constitution
dimitrovConstitution.description: The Georgi Dimitrov Constitution adopted, effective until 1971.

dimitrovDeath.title: Death of Georgi Dimitrov
dimitrovDeath.description: Dimitrov died and was displayed in the Georgi Dimitrov Mausoleum.

chervenkov.title: Rise of Chervenkov
chervenkov.description: Valko Chervenkov succeeded Dimitrov as general secretary until 1954.

zhivkovPower.title: Todor Zhivkov Takes Power
zhivkovPower.description: Zhivkov became head of the communist party, ruling for 35 years.

warsawPact.title: Bulgaria Joins Warsaw Pact
warsawPact.description: Bulgaria became a founding member of the Soviet-led military alliance.

aprilConspiracy.title: The April Conspiracy
aprilConspiracy.description: Failed coup attempt to overthrow Todor Zhivkov exposed.

czechoslovakia.title: Invasion of Czechoslovakia
czechoslovakia.description: Bulgarian troops joined Warsaw Pact invasion to crush the Prague Spring.

zhivkovConstitution.title: Zhivkov Constitution
zhivkovConstitution.description: New constitution in effect until the end of the People's Republic.

markov.title: Assassination of Georgi Markov
markov.description: Bulgarian dissident Georgi Markov assassinated in London with a poison-tipped umbrella, ordered by Bulgarian secret services.

cosmonaut.title: First Bulgarian Cosmonaut
cosmonaut.description: Georgi Ivanov launched into space on Mission Soyuz 33.

years1300.title: 1300 Years of Bulgaria
years1300.description: Massive military parade in Sofia celebrated 1300 years of statehood.

buzludzha.title: Buzludzha Inauguration
buzludzha.description: The Bulgarian Communist Party monument house inaugurated.

revivalProcess.title: Revival Process Begins
revivalProcess.description: Forced assimilation campaign against Turkish minority began.

greatExcursion.title: The Great Excursion
greatExcursion.description: Around 320,000 ethnic Turks expelled or fled Bulgaria.

regimeFall.title: Fall of the Regime
regimeFall.description: One day after Berlin Wall fell, Zhivkov was relieved of power.

endOfEra.title: End of an Era
endOfEra.description: People's Republic of Bulgaria officially ends, transition to democracy.

themes.title: Stories From a
themes.description: Each feature brings together a few objects, documents and photographs on a single subject, to tell one aspect of life behind the Iron Curtain.
themes.daily_life_desc: How ordinary Bulgarians lived under socialism — food queues, housing blocks, youth brigades and the rhythm of a controlled society.
themes.youth_desc: The Pioneers, Komsomol brigades, state schools and the systematic ideological formation of the next generation.
themes.sports_desc: Olympic medals, state sports clubs, organized holidays and the use of sport as a tool of national pride and Cold War competition.
themes.labor_desc: Factories, collectivized farms, five-year plans and the objects of a centrally planned socialist economy.
themes.politics_desc: The Communist Party, elections, ideology, ceremonies and the cult of personality that shaped Bulgarian political life.
themes.propaganda_desc: State-controlled newspapers, radio, television, posters and the machinery of ideological persuasion at work every day.
themes.tech_desc: Space industry, Soviet computing, telecommunications and the technological ambitions of a socialist state.
themes.military_desc: Bunkers, nuclear shelters, Warsaw Pact drills and the infrastructure of a country permanently prepared for war.
themes.surveillance_desc: The Darzhavna Sigurnost and its vast network of informants. One in six Bulgarians was monitored by the secret police.
themes.borders_desc: Forbidden zones, electrified fences, checkpoints and desperate escape attempts across the Bulgarian frontier.
themes.documents_desc: Official documents, censored newspapers, state publications, archives and printed materials from the communist era.

map.title: Bulgaria Behind the
map.description: Explore key locations of Cold War Bulgaria — monuments, bunkers, border zones and model socialist cities.
map.select_hint: Click on any marker to view details

sofia.description: Capital and power center. Home to the Largo Complex, the National Palace of Culture (NDK), and the Monument to the Soviet Army.

plovdiv.description: Second largest city. Famous for the Alyosha monument dominating the city from Bunardzhik Hill.

varna.description: Pearl of the Black Sea. Site of the massive Monument of Bulgarian-Soviet Friendship.

burgas.description: Key industrial port during the Cold War. Typical 1970s urban architecture and proximity to strategic refineries.

veliko-tarnovo.description: Former medieval capital and strategic base for visiting the Buzludzha Monument.

stara-zagora.description: City rebuilt on a perfect grid plan. Home to the Defenders of Stara Zagora Memorial.

ruse.description: The Little Vienna, major border point with Romania via the Friendship Bridge.

dimitrovgrad.description: The model socialist city of Bulgaria, built from scratch in the 1940s-50s by youth brigades.

pernik.description: Center of heavy industry and mining. Typical Palace of Culture.

pleven.description: Famous for the Pleven Epic 1877 Panorama, constructed by the USSR and Bulgaria in 1977.

shumen.description: Home to the gigantic Monument to the Founders of the Bulgarian State (1300 years).

sliven.description: Known for textile industry during socialist times.

dobrich.description: Agricultural center of the northeast. Soviet-style central square.

blagoevgrad.description: University city in the southwest near Greece and North Macedonia borders.

kardzhali.description: Southern city near the Kardzhali Dam, a major Cold War infrastructure project.

pazardzhik.description: Agricultural and industrial center with typical socialist-era factories.

kyustendil.description: Spa town near the Serbian border. Thermal baths state-owned during communist times.

gabrovo.description: Industrial hub known as the Bulgarian Manchester. Center of textile production.

smolyan.description: Mountain city built as a model socialist city in the 1960s-70s.

lovech.description: Famous for the Covered Bridge and proximity to Devetashka Cave (military storage).

montana.description: Northwestern city with mining heritage and typical brutalist architecture.

vidin.description: Border city on the Danube River. Strategic location during Cold War.

vratsa.description: City at the foot of the Balkan Mountains. Memorial complex dedicated to socialist-era heroes.

razgrad.description: Agricultural and industrial center in northeast Bulgaria.

silistra.description: Danube port city on the Romanian border.

targovishte.description: Center of the Bulgarian tobacco industry during communist times.

yambol.description: Industrial city in southeastern Bulgaria with military connections.

haskovo.description: Southern city near the Turkish and Greek borders.

bansko.description: A mountain town in the Pirin, developed under the regime as a state-run ski and tourism resort. It still holds a disused barracks whose military frescoes remain visible; the municipality is said to rent out premises there today.

borovets.description: Bulgaria's oldest mountain resort at the foot of Rila Mountain. Under communism, Borovets was a prestigious resort reserved for party officials and approved tourists.

belene.description: Labour camp on Persin Island in the Danube. Operated from 1949 to 1959, then reopened in 1985 to detain ethnic Turks who resisted the Revival Process.

kazanlak.description: A town in the Valley of the Roses, home to Arsenal, one of the country's main arms manufacturers since 1878, known for its Kalashnikov variants. The town also holds a Thracian tomb listed as World Heritage.

batak.description: A town in the Rhodopes, marked by the massacre of 1876: Ottoman troops killed several thousand Bulgarians there during the suppression of the April Uprising. The memorial and museum, built by the regime for the centenary in 1976, keep its memory.

buzludzha.description: Monument house of the Bulgarian Communist Party, opened in 1981 on Buzludzha Peak. Abandoned since 1989 and now derelict.

museum.title: The
museum.description: A curated collection of artifacts, documents and objects from Cold War Bulgaria. Collection currently being catalogued.
museum.banner_desc: Artifacts are currently being digitized and catalogued using Tropy. The full collection will be available soon.

reconstructions.title: Reconstructions
reconstructions.description: An experiment: what artificial intelligence makes of an era, working from real documents.
reconstructions.notice_title: These images are not archival documents
reconstructions.notice_body: Every image in this section is generated by artificial intelligence, drawing on period documents and photographs. They document nothing; they offer an interpretation. This section exists because AI has become a tool of our time, and testing it against historical material seemed more interesting than ignoring it. The collection's authentic documents are elsewhere on this site, where no generated image is published.

media.title: Stories in
media.description: Nine episodes on Cold War Bulgaria, from 1944 to the fall of the regime, plus a bonus on collecting today.
media.notice_title: Beta version, produced with AI assistance
media.notice_body: The voice and editing of these episodes are generated by artificial intelligence. The choice of subjects, the angle and the sources are my own. As with any AI-assisted work, some details may warrant independent verification. Beta version, currently available in French only.
media.no_audio: Your browser cannot play this audio file.

origins.title: Origins: Bulgaria in 1944
origins.desc: How the Kingdom of Bulgaria, an Axis ally, fell under Soviet control within weeks. Allied bombing, the Red Army's arrival, the collapse of the regime.

coup.title: The coup of 9 September 1944
coup.desc: The overthrow of the royal government by the Fatherland Front. The role of the partisans, that of the Red Army, and the installation of the new power.

terror.title: Vulko Chervenkov, Bulgaria's Little Stalin
terror.desc: The harshest years of the regime: political trials, camps, forced collectivisation. Bulgarian Stalinism from 1950 to 1956.

spearhead.title: Bulgaria, spearhead of the Soviet bloc
spearhead.desc: Alignment with Moscow: the Warsaw Pact, Comecon, and military integration into the Eastern bloc.

zealous.title: More zealous than Moscow
zealous.desc: Why Bulgaria was seen as the USSR's most compliant ally, and what that reputation actually covered.

economy.title: The price of loyalty
economy.desc: The economic side of the alliance: Soviet subsidies, oil at preferential rates, and what Bulgaria gained from it.

security.title: State Security and the Markov affair
security.desc: The Darzhavna Sigurnost, its ties to the KGB, its network of informers. The killing of Georgi Markov in London in 1978.

traffic.title: Intelligence and trafficking
traffic.desc: The clandestine work of the Bulgarian services abroad: arms, smuggling, foreign operations.

republic.title: A sixteenth Soviet republic?
republic.desc: Repeated requests to join the USSR, from the 1960s to the 1970s. A little-known chapter of Bulgarian-Soviet relations.

surplus.title: The surplus market today
surplus.desc: Where and how objects from communist Bulgaria still change hands: flea markets, military surplus, collectors. An episode about the present rather than the past.

publications.title: Books &
publications.description: Two freely downloadable works, drawn from the collection and from fieldwork.
publications.pub1_desc: 280 items from the collection, acquired between 2023 and 2025. Each object carries its own entry, from sports pennants to official papers: an illustrated inventory of material life in the People's Republic of Bulgaria.
publications.pub1_langs: Entries in English, Bulgarian and French
publications.pub2_title: The Secrets of the Reduta Hill
publications.pub2_desc: Nearly three years of research into an unassuming Sofia hill: Ottoman occupation, the world wars, the Cold War. Testimony, archives and a photographic survey, in a heritage-minded account that takes no side.
publications.pub2_langs: Available in French, Bulgarian and English

partners.title: They Support the
partners.description: Institutions, collectors and creators who contribute to preserving the memory of Cold War Bulgaria.

footer.description: An online multimedia museum documenting daily life, infrastructure, propaganda, and memory of the Cold War era in Bulgaria (1944–1989).
footer.notice_text: This website has been designated and registered online for cultural and historical purposes. It is in no way the tool of any political ideology.
